retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The patient with goiter can be managed by a family physician, internist, endocrinologist, ENT specialist, general or endocrine surgeon. If there is an indication for surgery, it should ideally be performed by experienced high-thyroid-volume surgeons.

retrieved_docs.title: Nontoxic Goiter -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Most nontoxic goiters can be managed with conservative care but large ones may require surgical excision. Others may require a trial of levothyroxine therapy. The pharmacist should educate the patient on symptoms of hyper and hypothyroidism which may occur following the use of levothyroxine and surgery, respectively.  These patients need to be followed up for several months to ensure that the TSH has stabilized. However, long term monitoring is necessary as some goiters may become hyperactive or become malignant. The primary care providers and nurse practitioners should examine these patients thoroughly so that no complication is missed. If the thyroid function tests fail to stabilize, the patient should be referred to an endocrinologist for further workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Nontoxic Goiter -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes for most patients with a goiter are excellent.

retrieved_docs.title: Nontoxic Goiter -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Goiters are not very common in the USA because of the fortification of the salt with iodine. However, when they do occur, their management can be difficult depending on their size and functional status. Thus, these lesions are best managed by an interprofessional team that includes clinicians, nurse specialists, pharmacists and surgeons. Goiters usually come to clinical attention when they are large; at this point, the clinician should first determine if the patient has symptoms and if the goiter is functioning.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Graves disease need an ophthalmology consult. For those who undergo thyroidectomy, lifelong treatment with levothyroxine is required. Pharmacists must review prescriptions, check for drug interactions, and educate patients. The interprofessional team must communicate with other members to ensure the patient receives the current standard of care treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: The personnel required to perform a thyroidectomy typically include: Primary surgeon Surgical assistant (not an absolute requirement) Surgical technician or operating room nurse Circulating or operating room nurse Anesthesia personnel

retrieved_docs.title: Plummer Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Plummer disease is a disorder that affects many organs due to the development of hyperthyroidism. Its manifestations are diverse and can be due to hyperthyroidism or the mass effect of the goiter. Hence, the disorder is best managed by an interprofessional team consisting of an endocrinologist, an internist, a radiologist, a nuclear medicine specialist, a surgeon, nursing staff, and pharmacists. Plummer disease's natural history is well-researched and documented. The thyroid gland transforms itself from diffuse hyperplasia to euthyroid nodules or nodules, and then nodules with autonomy develop on the background of the enlarged thyroid gland that may also contain nonfunctional nodules. It can start as low TSH and subclinical hyperthyroidism and may eventually lead to thyrotoxicosis if not treated. It is usually seen after the age of 50. The condition is best treated with surgery or radioactive iodine. Regular follow-ups to assess post-operative hypothyroidism or disease recurrence are required for the rest of the patient's life. Lifelong antithyroid therapy is necessary if a patient is a poor candidate for surgery or refuses surgery or radioactive iodine. The other alternative is percutaneous ethanol injection of the hyperactive nodule.

retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with goiter commonly present to outpatient facilities with a variety of complaints. Goiter encompasses many different causes and morbidities, and accurate diagnosis is essential for the proper treatment approach. Goiter, either simple or nodular, or either nontoxic or toxic can majorly impact a patient's quality of life and well-being and can have long-term physical and cosmetic health effects. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of goiter, reviews different causes and types, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team from various specialties in evaluating and treating goiter.

retrieved_docs.title: Nontoxic Goiter -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Correct iodine deficiency (not a problem in the US as salt is supplemented with iodine) Treat patients with levothyroxine if they have had a prior lobectomy After surgery, monitor patients for hypothyroidism for 3-4 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of goiter. Outline the evaluation of patients who present with a goiter. Describe the management options available for goiter. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Toxic Goiter -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with diffuse goiters are advised to be evaluated by endocrinologists to undergo more detailed testing. Teamwork between primary care physicians, radiologists, surgeons, and endocrine specialists should be coordinated to achieve better outcomes. Pharmacists should also counsel the patients on the potential side effects of the medications used for their treatment so they can be aware of the findings and report them to their doctors promptly. Treatment approach steps should be discussed in advance, and the patients should be provided with details of the expected advantages versus disadvantages of the different treatment choices. On several occasions, the physicians caring for those patients must communicate closely to prepare the patients for the next steps to guarantee better compliance and results.

retrieved_docs.title: Substernal Goiter -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of substernal goiter in the general population is unknown due to the lack of epidemiological studies. However, with the widespread use of imaging, more cases of substernal goiter are expected to come to clinical attention. Substernal goiter is more commonly diagnosed after age 50 and occurs four times more frequently in females. [4] [7] A recent surgical series reported a substernal goiter rate of 7% among patients who underwent thyroid surgery. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common cause of goiters worldwide is iodine deficiency that affects an estimated 2.2 billion people. [2] The prevalence and incidence of goiter are based on the degree of iodine deficiency. With mild iodine deficiency, the incidence of goiter is 5% to 20%. With a moderate deficiency, the prevalence increases to 20% to 30%, and with severe iodine deficiency, the incidence increases to greater than 30%. Even with the use of iodine, there has been an increase in the incidence of thyroid nodules. It is not clear the increase in prevalence represents a true increase or an increased detection. One of the causes of this could be the increased utilization of radiological imaging and more frequent screening with ultrasound.

retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid hormonal function and the growth rate of the thyroid gland reflect the clinical presentation of a goiter. If the patient has hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism, he or she will have the signs or symptoms of hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. The vast majority of patients will be euthyroid. The growth rate is usually slow and patients accommodate the enlarging thyroid at is a usual anatomic place very well without any sign or symptoms except for esthetical complaints or if the thyroid grows inferiorly to the thoracic cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Substernal Goiter -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the widespread use of chest imaging and cancer screening, substernal goiter is expected to be diagnosed more frequently and earlier. Patients with substernal goiter should be referred to an endocrinologist for evaluation and management. If surgery is indicated, a consultation with a surgeon experienced in thyroid procedures is recommended. For cases requiring an extra-cervical approach, a thoracic surgery consultation is necessary. Surgery for substernal goiter should be performed at tertiary institutions with expertise in thyroid surgery and its associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Some palpable nodules will not be radiologically distinct from the surrounding thyroid parenchyma and will therefore not meet the strict definition for thyroid nodules. [1] In these cases, follow-up should be based on individual risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2549
retrieved_docs.content: The term استئصال الدرقية is the Arabic name for thyroidectomy, a surgical procedure that involves removing all or part of the thyroid gland; it may be performed as a partial or a complete thyroidectomy depending on the underlying thyroid condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor in the clinical management of تمزق, thyroid disorders, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that it may be used when documenting or planning treatment for each of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a type of diiodotyrosine, a compound that participates in thyroid hormone synthesis, and they are employed to inhibit thyroid hormone production in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
retrieved_docs.content: Triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin) is a thyroid hormone that is synthesized and secreted by the thyroid gland (štítná žláza). The štítná žláza is also the primary anatomical site where thyroid insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition characterized by reduced production of thyroid hormones, is identified. Recognizing the relationship between trijodthyronin and the štítná žláza, as well as the gland’s role in قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة, is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of disorders affecting thyroid hormone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2173
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy), reflecting its categorization within endocrine pathologies and the common surgical intervention used in its treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid gland (štítná žláza) is the organ that synthesizes thyroid hormones (hormony štítné žlázy) and specifically produces triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin). When the function of the thyroid gland is impaired, it can result in hypothyroidism (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition marked by insufficient hormone production. Recognizing the relationship between the thyroid gland, its hormones, and hypothyroidism is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Karbimazol, also known as carbimazole, is classified as an antithyroid preparation and is used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors comprise a category that includes the subclass thyreostatika, which are agents specifically related to the function of the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). Both thyreostatika and the thyroid hormone trijodthyronin (triiodothyronine) are directly associated with the štítná žláza, reflecting their roles in modulating thyroid activity. The štítná žláza is also identified as the anatomical finding site for قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism), underscoring the clinical relevance of these substances and the gland in the diagnosis and management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified within the broader category of medications and vaccines, reflecting their role as therapeutic agents used to manage thyroid disorders. These preparations encompass specific compounds such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin, which are individual antithyroid drugs that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis. By being part of the medication and vaccine group, antithyroid preparations are recognized for their clinical importance in treating conditions like hyperthyroidism, and their inclusion of agents such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin highlights the diversity of pharmacologic options available within this therapeutic class.
